schools.summary: The hour now ‘come’ (after so many ‘not yet,’ 2:4; 7:6); ‘his own’ are the believers; ‘loved unto the end’ = constancy to death; most place the scene at the Last Supper (Thursday eve), Gill/Lightfoot earlier at Bethany.
Augustine: ‘unto the end’ (eis telos) means his love led him to death — and to himself, ‘the end of the law’ (Rom 10:4).
Chrysostom/Theophylact: Christ teaches virtue by action, not words only — the foot-washing is meekness made visible (Mt 11:29; 1 Cor 11:1).
Calvin: a visible sign that his love is firm and lasting — death itself would not quench it.

sides.position: at the Last Supper, the night before the crucifixion

schools.summary: ‘Supper being ended’ is a mistranslation — ‘during supper’ (ginomenou), the meal still going (vv.4, 26); the devil ‘put it into the heart’ of Judas (Lk 22:3; 6:70), John’s ‘already’ pertinent.
Calvin: John notes the betrayal here to show Christ’s wonderful patience — he washes the feet even of the traitor, choosing the time near death for this last act.

In full consciousness of his supreme dignity — all things given him (Mt 28:18), come from and going to God (16:28) — he stoops to a slave’s task, the kenosis of Phil 2:6-7; the height measures the depth of the condescension.
Calvin and others: not in ignorance but in full knowledge of his greatness he humbles himself, leaving a lesson of humility to cure the disciples’ ambition.
Each step is the servile preparation of a slave — laying aside the outer garments, girding with a towel — the Master deliberately taking the servant’s part.
Calvin and others read it as an acted parable of the incarnation/kenosis: he lays aside the tokens of majesty to clothe himself as a servant (Phil 2:7), prefiguring the stripping of the cross.
Foot-washing was the lowest slave’s office (dusty sandalled feet, water provided by a host, Lk 7:44); that Jesus pours, washes, and wipes each disciple’s feet inverts every expectation.
Calvin and others: the act enacts the love of v.1 (love that kneels) and pictures cleansing — the daily washing the believer needs (opened in v.10).
Peter’s startled ‘Dost thou my feet wash?’ (emphatic pronouns); the order (Peter first or after another) is uncertain and unimportant.
Augustine: who would not fear to have his feet washed by the Son of God? Peter’s audacity is reverence; Origen: the physician washes the most unclean first, Peter last.
Calvin: the protest, though seemingly modest, forgets that obedience outweighs honour — a humility that, refusing Christ’s command, becomes pride.
A gentle rebuke (emphatic ‘I… thou’): Peter does not now grasp the symbolic act but ‘shall know hereafter’ (meta tauta) — chiefly in the explanation of vv.14-17, known ‘by experience.’
Calvin: we must obey Christ even without perceiving the reason — to refuse obedience for lack of a reason is haughtiness; Wesley: enough to love and obey now, knowing hereafter.

Peter’s vehement ‘never’ shows the impulsive, self-confident disciple; Christ’s ‘if I wash thee not, thou hast no part with me’ lifts the act to its spiritual sense — washing in his blood/Spirit; ‘the first condition of discipleship is self-surrender.’
Calvin: reverence turned to obstinacy — ignorance followed by obstinacy; misplaced respect cannot excuse refusing Christ’s command.
Peter swings from refusal to excess — ‘not my feet only’ — fearing to lose communion; ‘a moment ago too much, now too little’ (Dods); ardour half-grasping the spiritual meaning.
Calvin: necessity tames him to yield, yet he errs through thoughtlessness, undervaluing the cleansing already received; Wesley: well-meant but ignorant earnestness.
Two verbs: the ‘bathed’ man (leloumenos, regenerated/justified, ‘clean every whit’) needs only to ‘wash’ (nipsasthai) his feet — the daily defilements of his walk, not a fresh regeneration.
Augustine: ‘but not all’ — the betrayer; outward washing cannot cleanse a heart harbouring betrayal (explained in v.11).

John explains ‘not all clean’ as Judas — his feet washed but his heart, harbouring the betrayal (v.2), unclean; outward grace cannot cleanse an unrenewed soul, and Christ knew the traitor from the first.
He reclines again and asks the searching ‘Know ye what I have done?’ (perhaps imperative, ‘Consider’) — to direct them to the meaning he is about to give, the act done to all twelve.
Augustine: he begins to fulfil his promise to teach Peter; Alcuin finds in his taking his garments again an image of the risen Lord resuming his glory.
Calvin: he discloses his intention — that all the godly follow him in not grudging the lowliest service to their brethren.
‘Master’ (Teacher) and ‘Lord’ (sovereign reverence) were the honoured rabbinic titles rightly given him; he accepts them — ‘so I am’ — establishing the greatness on which the coming lesson rests.

Argument from greater to less: if the Lord washed their feet, they ‘owe’ one another the lowliest service; most (Barnes, Wesley) hold it commands humble love, not a literal rite (no apostle is recorded washing feet).
Calvin: pride hinders the equality that should exist; the exalted Lord stoops to shame the proud who think themselves above serving the brethren.

debates.question: Does this institute a literal foot-washing ordinance?

sides.position: no — it commands humble mutual service, of which the act is a parable

schools.summary: The example (hypodeigma) is in the principle, not the specific act — ‘according as’ I have done; Christ is the believer’s pattern (1 Pet 5:3; 2:21) in grace and humility, not in his unrepeatable works.
Calvin: not all his actions are for imitation (against keeping Lent by his fast) — we copy the spirit, not every outward deed.
The proverb (Mt 10:24; Jn 15:20): the servant is not above his lord, nor the apostle above his sender — aimed to suppress lordly ambition; expect the world’s treatment too (Mt 10:25).
Augustine: he said it because he had washed their feet, ‘the Master of humility both by word and example.’
Knowing these things confers no blessing; the beatitude is ‘if ye do them’ — happiness lies in the doing, not the knowing, a warning against mistaking grasp of a duty for its discharge.
Calvin and others: knowledge of humble love without practice only increases responsibility; the blessing rests on the obedient, not the learned.

themes: ‘happy are ye if ye do them’

schools.summary: ‘Not of you all’ excepts Judas; ‘whom I have chosen’ = chosen to apostleship (6:70; Lk 6:13); the betrayal fulfils Ps 41:9 — the table-companion who ‘lifted up his heel.’
Calvin: Christ names the traitor to render Judas inexcusable and to keep the others from being overthrown by his ruin; Chrysostom: the horror of falling to the depths.
He foretells the betrayal ‘before it come’ so that its fulfilment may confirm their faith that ‘I am he’ (8:24) — proof of his knowledge of the heart and divine mission.
Calvin: a reprobate among them should not discourage but confirm faith — when Scripture agrees with experience, belief is established.
The mission-saying (Mt 10:40) set amid the betrayal: to receive the sent apostle is to receive Christ, and through him the Father — comfort that they will be received by some though rejected by many.
Calvin: set to guard against the scandal of Judas — the apostles’ ministry stands in unbroken union with Christ and the Father; Wesley: ‘I put my own honour upon you.’
Chrysostom: what can equal receiving Christ and the Father? — the dignity conferred on the servants of God.

themes: guard against the scandal of Judas

schools.summary: His trouble springs from his true humanity — wounded by a professed friend’s treason; he ‘testified’ (spoke earnestly) and now states plainly what he had hinted: ‘one of you shall betray me.’
Augustine: troubled ‘in spirit, not flesh,’ by his own will, as at Lazarus’ grave (11:33), being about to single out the traitor; Chrysostom: troubled that the traitor is cut off from the promises.
The eleven, wholly surprised, look one on another, unable to name the traitor (the Synoptics: each asks ‘Lord, is it I?’) — proof Judas had perfectly concealed his purpose.
Calvin and others: hypocrisy may lie hidden in the closest fellowship; their suspecting themselves rather than each other marks humble, tender consciences.

The reclining custom (propped on the left arm) put ‘the disciple whom Jesus loved’ — John himself, veiling his name in modesty — next to Christ, resting on his breast (en tō kolpō), the place of nearest fellowship.
Bede/Augustine: the bosom signifies secret — ‘the secret chamber of wisdom’ — a sign of the deep doctrines John would later reveal, the eagle drawing from Christ’s breast.
The cautious-but-curious Peter, at some distance, beckons (neuei) to John — nearest Jesus and his lifelong companion (1:40; Acts 3:1) — to ask who the traitor is; a vivid eye-witness touch.
John ‘falls back’ (anapesōn, a different word from v.23) onto Jesus’ breast to ask privately, in a whisper unheard by the others (v.28) — reverence (‘Lord’) joined to closeness.

The dipped ‘sop’ (psōmion) was a token of intimacy (Ruth 2:14; Mk 14:20); unusual to hand it, the others took it as favour or an errand and missed the sign; Christ answered John ‘in his ear,’ sparing Judas.

Calvin: the sign revealed Judas to John alone (a witness for later), Christ delaying the public exposure; Augustine: the bread was a sign, not poison — the guilt lay in the heart receiving love while plotting murder.

themes: love’s last gift to the traitor

schools.summary: Satan (only here in John) enters to confirm the deed already in Judas’ heart (v.2; Lk 22:3) at the moment he takes the pledge of love while plotting murder; Lightfoot’s type — the second Adam betrayed by one Satan first entered.
‘Do quickly’ is neither permission nor command (Wesley) but ‘why delay?’ — Christ, master of his hour, will not have it drawn out; the sop was not the Lord’s Supper (Clarke).

themes: grace refused, the door to Satan

schools.summary: John’s comment that none at the table grasped the meaning of ‘do quickly’ — the traitor revealed to John alone, the rest not perceiving Judas’ heart.
Calvin: John had not yet told the others, or all were struck dumb; few in the Church discern the hypocrites the Lord condemns.

Judas held the bag (12:6), so the others took ‘do quickly’ as an errand — to buy for the feast or give to the poor; the office that hid his theft now masks his treachery (Clarke notes the chronology bearing).

Judas goes ‘immediately out’ (euthys), the deed set in motion; ‘and it was night’ is both a note of time and a symbolic darkness — he leaves the light of the world (8:12) for the night of the hour and of his soul.

themes: apostasy as going into the night

schools.summary: With Judas gone, the death is set in motion; Christ calls it glory (‘now is glorified,’ aorist, 4×) — the cross the consummation of the Son’s and the Father’s glory (12:23).
Calvin: he supports the weak disciples by showing he triumphs in the cross over Satan, sin, and death; Origen: the going-out of Judas opens the glorifying.

themes: ‘now is the Son of man glorified’

schools.summary: The future glory of the Father’s throne: if God is glorified in the Son’s death, God will ‘straightway’ glorify the Son ‘in himself’ (17:5) — cross then quickly ascension, with no postponement.
Calvin: the Son glorifies the Father in the cross, and the Father makes the Son partaker of that glory — illustrious honour displayed beyond the brief ignominy.

themes: mutual glory of Father and Son

schools.summary: The tender ‘little children’ (teknia, only here in the Gospel, common in 1 John) frames the warning of departure; ‘whither I go ye cannot come’ — said to the Jews (7:33; 8:21) but now in love, not judgment.
Augustine: ‘yet a little while’ — he will be with them forty days after the resurrection before ascending; not glorified away from them at once.
Calvin: warning and comfort together — he restrains their eagerness to follow, yet the gentle name shows his care for their welfare.
‘New’ not in substance (Lev 19:18 commanded neighbour-love) but in standard and degree — ‘as I have loved you,’ a love that washes feet and lays down life; a badge of discipleship.
Augustine: new because it renews us, dividing from the old man; Calvin: the exhortation crowning the consolation — testify by mutual love.

debates.question: Why is the commandment to love called ‘new’?

sides.position: new in standard/degree — ‘as I have loved you’ (the cross)
new because it renews us / belongs to the new covenant

schools.summary: Mutual love (not power, knowledge, or profession) is the badge by which all men recognise Christ’s disciples — the credential the world can read.
Calvin and others: love after Christ’s pattern (v.34) is the public testimony of belonging to him — comfort where it is, rebuke where it is wanting.
Peter, unable to bear separation, asks ‘whither goest thou?’; ‘thou canst not follow now, but afterwards’ points to his future martyrdom (21:18-19) — he is not yet ready for the way of the cross.
Augustine: ‘follow afterwards’ = follow in death, by martyrdom; Calvin: the deferral is a tender promise, not a refusal, restraining Peter’s untimely ardour.
Peter’s sincere but presumptuous self-confidence — demanding the ‘now’ and pledging to lay down his life, overrating his own strength, ignorant of his weakness.
Calvin: he trusts too much to his own strength, forgetting that fidelity is grace’s gift, not the flesh’s power — his boast measures his blindness.
Christ echoes Peter’s boast as a question, then foretells precisely the opposite of his offered martyrdom — three denials before cockcrow, which the night fulfilled to the letter.

Calvin: the rebuke teaches how frail human strength is when self-reliant; the plain prophecy is mercy — it will humble Peter and, fulfilled, confirm faith (v.19); yet the ‘afterwards’ (v.36) still stands.

themes: the denier to become the martyr
